pathway_title: Inguinal hernia in adults: primary care threshold-led referral pathway
scope: Adults; UK/Scotland-facing primary care and surgical referral interface
principles: Prioritise urgency triage first (emergency vs urgent vs routine vs watchful waiting).
Use watchful waiting for appropriately selected minimally symptomatic reducible cases.
Give explicit safety-net advice for incarceration/obstruction/strangulation symptoms.

stepwise_pathway.name: Confirm likely diagnosis and immediate risk
actions: Clinical history and groin examination (standing/cough impulse/reducibility where possible).
Identify red flags: severe persistent pain, irreducible tender lump, vomiting/obstructive symptoms, systemic illness.
If red flags present: same-day emergency pathway (A&E/surgical emergency).

bands.threshold: all suspected groin hernias in women; irreducible/partially reducible groin hernia
symptomatic reducible inguinal hernia (typically men) or diagnostic uncertainty
minimally symptomatic/asymptomatic reducible inguinal hernia (usually men), after informed discussion
bands.action: manage in primary care with safety-netting and re-access plan

stepwise_pathway.name: Primary care management while awaiting or instead of referral
actions: For non-operative candidates or those declining surgery, consider truss/support garment discussion.
Optimise comorbidity and frailty factors where elective surgery may be considered.
Document shared decision making (including risks/benefits of watchful waiting vs surgery).

stepwise_pathway.name: Imaging policy
actions: Do not request routine ultrasound for clinically obvious uncomplicated inguinal hernia.
Consider imaging only where diagnosis is uncertain or alternative pathology is likely (per local pathway).

stepwise_pathway.name: Referral information payload
referral_payload: Side/site, reducibility, symptom burden and functional impact
Any red-flag episodes (irreducibility, obstruction symptoms, severe pain)
Sex and pregnancy status where relevant
Comorbidity/frailty and patient preference (including willingness for surgery)

implementation_notes: No inguinal-hernia-specific SIGN guidance identified; Scottish implementation should use Right Decisions national/board pathways.
NICE CKS topic is relevant but was not extractable in this environment; include direct CKS verification at local clinical sign-off.
Where local board/ICB policy adds commissioning criteria, apply those locally without changing emergency/urgent safety thresholds.

evidence_confidence.rationale: High consistency for triage thresholds (emergency, urgent in women, watchful waiting in selected men) across NHS Scotland, UK specialty, and EBI sources. Confidence reduced by inability to directly extract NICE CKS clinical text in this run and by local policy variation in elective criteria.
